claim: Trust engineering is a discipline combining decision design, expectation management, trust infrastructure, and trust assurance to enable agentic AI in production while preserving human oversight and accountability
actionable: When deploying agentic systems, structure decisions around four pillars: design workflows around error costs, set user expectations, implement monitoring/guardrails, and define governance/escalation paths.
source_title: Opening Keynote: Engineering for Trust in the Era of Agentic AI

claim: AI incident database shows exponential rise in verified AI-caused harms since generative AI adoption, driven by hallucinations and malicious prompt injection; agentic tools increase blast radius of these failures
actionable: Assume your agentic system will fail; design workflows to detect and contain failures rather than trying to prevent them entirely.
source_title: Opening Keynote: Engineering for Trust in the Era of Agentic AI

claim: Distinction between task-level efficiency and operational effectiveness: automating processes without redesigning them moves 'faster and faster in place'; AI gains must be paired with workflow redesign
actionable: When deploying AI agents, redesign workflows to leverage the time saved; don't just replace human steps with agent steps without rethinking the process.
source_title: Opening Keynote: Engineering for Trust in the Era of Agentic AI

claim: Higher education transfer credit workflow: agentic system was 90% accurate but failed predictably on foreign transcripts and trimester systems; fix was workflow redesign (rules-based routing) not more AI
actionable: Identify where your agent fails systematically (via evals); then redesign the workflow to route those cases to human review rather than improving the agent.
source_title: Opening Keynote: Engineering for Trust in the Era of Agentic AI

claim: Decision design principle: cost of false negatives and false positives are different; cancer screening (missed diagnosis vs. false alarm) and agentic systems require threshold tuning, not automation of everything
actionable: For each agentic task, define the cost of each error type (false positive vs. false negative); use that to set the threshold for automation vs. human review.
source_title: Opening Keynote: Engineering for Trust in the Era of Agentic AI

claim: Expectation management: design signals that tell users how to safely interact with agentic systems (e.g., Waymo pedestrian light, Kohler faucet app showing good/bad angles); critical for user trust and error reduction
actionable: When building agentic UIs, include visual/textual cues telling users the safe/valid inputs and what the system can/cannot do; prevent misuse via guidance rather than restrictions.
source_title: Opening Keynote: Engineering for Trust in the Era of Agentic AI

claim: LLM large context windows (2M tokens) can increase hallucinations if conflicting/outdated sources mixed in; 'pink elephant' phenomenon where models fixate on things told not to do; design prompts to isolate relevant information
actionable: Don't dump entire knowledge base into context; carefully curate relevant sources and avoid mixing conflicting information; test with adversarial/outdated data.
source_title: Opening Keynote: Engineering for Trust in the Era of Agentic AI

claim: Context mingling: agentic systems can unintentionally combine information sources inappropriately (e.g., energy marketing + transmission teams sharing data → collusion risk); design agents with data access guardrails
actionable: Audit agent tool access; ensure agents cannot combine data sources that would be illegal/unethical if humans combined them (insider trading, price collusion, privacy violations).
source_title: Opening Keynote: Engineering for Trust in the Era of Agentic AI

claim: United Airlines: GenAI personalized flight delay messages for 50% of passengers (vs. 5% before) by using storytellers team to review/pre-approve messages; humans in the loop by design improves trust and coverage
actionable: For high-visibility agentic outputs (customer communication), route through human approval step rather than fully automating; this increases user trust and regulatory defensibility.
source_title: Opening Keynote: Engineering for Trust in the Era of Agentic AI

claim: System cards (model cards, AI nutritional labels): document upfront de-risking, known limitations, in-production monitoring metrics, and fallback/escalation plans; OpenAI's ChatGPT system card is 70 pages; examine table of contents to understand constraints
actionable: Create system cards for all production agentic systems answering: what's been done to de-risk? How will you know if it breaks? What's the off switch?
source_title: Opening Keynote: Engineering for Trust in the Era of Agentic AI

claim: Prompt injection risk in agentic tools: even if website is not malicious, compromised site can inject instructions into agent's search results; broader agent autonomy increases attack surface
actionable: For agents that search or scrape web content, sanitize and validate inputs before passing to LLM; use guardrails to detect injected instructions in search results.
source_title: Opening Keynote: Engineering for Trust in the Era of Agentic AI

claim: Humans hold machines to higher error standards than other humans; study by Harvard Business School researcher Julian De Freitas: self-driving car blamed more than human driver for same accident (not due to fault, just bias)
actionable: Recognize that users will be harsher on agent errors; design UX to make errors transparent and recoverable; consider naming strategies (avoid human-like names) to reduce emotional negative reactions.
source_title: Opening Keynote: Engineering for Trust in the Era of Agentic AI

claim: No universal moral frame: Moral Machine experiment showed Eastern cultures prioritize older lives even over larger number; North American contexts prioritize saving most lives; ethics can't be solved by algorithm alone
actionable: When designing agentic systems for global use, don't assume one ethical rule applies everywhere; involve regional stakeholders in decision-making thresholds and tradeoffs.
source_title: Opening Keynote: Engineering for Trust in the Era of Agentic AI

claim: Red/yellow/green governance: red (don't do), green (no red tape), yellow (stop and think); treat high-risk AI use cases (financial impact, sensitive data) as yellow; define ethical nightmares upfront
actionable: Classify agentic use cases by risk (red=banned, yellow=requires approval, green=approved); for yellow, document specific harms you want to prevent and design guardrails for them.
source_title: Opening Keynote: Engineering for Trust in the Era of Agentic AI
